coredata.dc:title: Graphene hybrid nanostructures based screen-printed sensor employed in the glyphosate electrocatalytic determination in the real sample

coredata.dc:description: This work describes the synthesis characterization and application of innovative CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures obtained by direct reaction between graphene oxide, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CDot(N)), and copper (II) nitrate. The hybrid nanostructures were characterized by spectroscopies methods such as Raman, ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and electrochemical techniques. A sensitive electrochemical sensor to determine glyphosate in the real sample has been constructed by a printed carbon electrode modified with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. Determination of the pesticide glyphosate (glyph) has been performed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 5.5 and in real water samples by differential pulse voltammetry technique (DPV). The printed carbon electrode modified with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures presented a detection limit of 1.97 nmol.L−1 (0.3 µg.L−1). The modified, printed carbon electrode determined glyphosate in real samples, with recovery ranging from 95.2 % to 114%. Other pesticides didn't present significant interference in the glyphosate determination.

head.ce:title: Graphene hybrid nanostructures based screen-printed sensor employed in the glyphosate electrocatalytic determination in the real sample

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes the synthesis characterization and application of innovative CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures obtained by direct reaction between graphene oxide, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CDot(N)), and copper (II) nitrate. The hybrid nanostructures were characterized by spectroscopies methods such as Raman, ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and electrochemical techniques. A sensitive electrochemical sensor to determine glyphosate in the real sample has been constructed by a printed carbon electrode modified with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. Determination of the pesticide glyphosate (glyph) has been performed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 5.5 and in real water samples by differential pulse voltammetry technique (DPV). The printed carbon electrode modified with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures presented a detection limit of 1.97 nmol.L−1 (0.3 µg.L−1). The modified, printed carbon electrode determined glyphosate in real samples, with recovery ranging from 95.2 % to 114%. Other pesticides didn't present significant interference in the glyphosate determination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are chemical substances used to combat and eliminate pests and diseases across various sectors [1]. In this way, pesticides are classified based on their application, such as insecticides, fungicides, and herbicides [2]. Due to population growth, millions of tons of herbicides have been constantly used to control crop pests and enhance productivity. Among the different classes of herbicides, the organophosphate class [3] has been widely used in agriculture due to its broad spectrum of action. In this sense, glyphosate, commonly called Roundup and RangerPro, N-(phosphonomethyl) glycine, is considered the most used herbicide in the world [4,5], across more than 140 countries, covering 350 million hectares of crops, with an annual turnover of 11 billion USD worldwide [6] mainly in fighting against pests in soybean, tomato, and strawberry plantations. However, plants absorb only about 3-5% of applied glyphosate, with the excess released into the soil, rivers, and air causing severe damage to the health of living beings, often resulting in death, neural diseases, muscular and cardiovascular [7]. Therefore, developing on-site monitoring and diagnosis sensors is crucial to removing glyphosate from the water and protecting individuals' health from its adverse effects.

Glyphosate (glyph) is commonly detected using various analytical techniques, including spectroscopic techniques such as Fourier Transformed InfraRed (FTIR) and Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis), chromatographic methods such as HPLC, GC, and Mass Spectrometry (MS) [8–11] and electrochemistry [12]. Different regulatory agencies have set allowable levels of glyphosate in water [13]. The European Union (EU) has set a maximum permissible level of 59 nmol L⁻¹ (100 μg L⁻¹), the United States Environmental Protection Agency at 4.15 μmol L⁻¹ (700 μg L⁻¹), and the Brazilian regulatory quantifying glyphosate at low concentrations is essential to prevent environmental pollution and address emerging health concerns.

Electrochemical sensors are a very effective technique for detecting pesticides due to present resolution, selectivity, repeatability, sensitivity, stability, linearity, range, and reproducibility [14]. However, despite being widely used techniques and having advantages over electrochemistry for the analysis of pesticides, they have limitations such as low selectivity for complex samples, limitations related to solvents, or the need to prepare the sample before analysis. In addition, these techniques cannot be miniaturized in the form of portable kits, making rapid or outside-laboratory analyses difficult [15]. Thus, electrochemical sensors can overcome the limitations presented by other analytical techniques because they have high sensitivity and selectivity and a low detection limit in aqueous solutions. Furthermore, sensors can be improved by modifying the electrode surfaces with different nanomaterials [16], thus being able to detect analytes in low concentrations (in the order of micromolar to nanomolar), being selective in detection among interferents, and varying their analytical signal with the minimum increase in the concentration of pesticides. Among the classes of nanomaterials, hybrid nanomaterials stand out. These are formed by combining different nanomaterials, aiming to improve the properties that the nanomaterials present in isolation and promote the emergence of new properties, such as mass transfer, good conductivity, high active site numbers, and acid-basic properties onto the surface. Thus, hybrid nanomaterials based on carbon allotropes perform excellently in developing electrochemical sensors [17,18].

Carbon nanostructures, mainly graphene derivatives, such as graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and carbon quantum dots (CDot), have been widely used in the construction of electrochemical sensors due to their different functional groups, semi-filled orbitals, favoring good electron transfer and ease of modification of their surface [19–21]. Graphene is characterized by having a 2D structure, with carbons hybridized in sp2 bonds, which enhances the electrical properties of graphene, allowing a good movement of electrons and improving the electrical conductivity and properties [22]. GO has a different structure from graphene because of its hybridization in sp2 and sp3 bonds due to different oxygenated functional groups, directly influencing the electrocatalytic properties. As for rGO, it has fewer sp3 hybridization bonds than GO due to a restoration of sp2 bonds [23].

The combination of graphene species with other materials results in the formation of graphene nanohybrids that combine the characteristics of the isolated materials in a single material. In addition, the synergistic effect between the nanomaterials favors the emergence of other properties not presented by the isolated materials, such as excellent electron and mass transfer, a more significant number of acidic/ basic active sites, chemical stability, and high surface area, making these nanohybrids excellent materials to be used as substrates in the development of electrochemical sensors [24–30].

Carbon quantum dots (CDot) are a new class of carbon allotropes with fluorescent properties, biocompatible, size less than 10 nm, and 0D structure. Depending on the route synthetic used (top-down or bottom-up) [31], the CDot can present an amorphous structure with C-C sp3 bonds or a crystalline structure with C-C sp2 bonds. Thus, by varying the method of obtaining CDot, they can be applied in several different ways, such as in chemical sensing of heavy metals (Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+), in bioimaging, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, optoelectronics, drug carrier and has an antimicrobial function [32,33]. The insertion of other atoms in the structure, such as fluoride, sulfur, and nitrogen atoms, promotes the enhancement of existing properties and facilitates the emergence of new properties by providing electrochemical sensors [34–36]. Because of quantum confirmation and different functional groups on their surface, mainly hydroxyl and carboxylic groups [29,37], CDot has been used as a reducing agent in the obtention of metallic nanoparticles employed in electrochemical sensors [38–40] Besides, many hybrid nanostructures have been obtained by combining synergic effects between different carbon dots and other nanomaterials. These presented excellent electrocatalytic responses for many species in real samples [41], mainly because new Lewis and BrΦnsted-Lowry acids and basic sites emerged.

Copper nanostructures have been widely used in electrocatalytic processes because they combine the properties of copper nanoparticles with the properties of different nanostructures, improving the transfer of electrons at the electrode-solution interface. Such electrocatalytic properties of copper nanostructures are probably the result of the oxygen-containing crystalline structure, quantum confinement effects, and distribution of electrons in semi-filled d orbitals with lower energy. Comparing bulk and CuO nanomaterials, these have greater surface area, optical absorption, chemical reactivity, and excellent chemical stability, among other properties. This means that copper nanostructures have a wide range of applications, whether in catalysis, sensors or biosensors, energy storage devices, biocidal agents, and solar energy converters [42–44].
This work describes developing electrochemical sensors for determining glyphosate in real samples by modifying screen-printed electrodes with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures.

The following analytical grade reagents were used: oleylamine, technical grade 70% (Sigma); copper(II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2Aldrich); hexane (Aldrich); sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Aldrich); sodium chloride (NaCl, Aldrich); sodium nitrate (NaNO₃, Aldrich); sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄, Aldrich); glyphosate (Aldrich); methylcarbamate (Aldrich); aldicarb (Aldrich); fenitrothion (Aldrich); and absolute ethanol (Synth), dried with 0.3 nm molecular sieves (Metrohm).
The absorption spectra in the UV-Vis region were obtained on a Hewlett-Packard HP-8453 spectrometer using a quartz cuvette.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures were obtained using an X-ray source operated at 12 KeV, 15 mA from a VSW HA 100 using Al anode as the X-ray source. The binding energy of elements has been corrected, considering that the Cls peak lies at 284.6 eV by The Shirley method [45].
HR-TEM micrography has been obtained using a JEOL JEM 2100. The samples were prepared on CF300-Ni-50 Carbon Film, 300 Mesh.

A Rigaku diffractometer has obtained X-ray diffraction (XRD) to investigate the structural characteristics of the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures using a monochromatic Cu-Kα source (λ = 0.154 nm) at scanning angle (2θ) in the range of 5° to 90°.

The electrochemical measurements were done on Potentiostat-Galvanostat PGSTAT20 / Autolab using an electrochemical cell containing a working electrode (carbon screen-printed electrodes (PCE) bare and modified), an auxiliary electrode (platinum wire), and a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl 3M KCl). The measurements were performed in 20 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS), 0.1 mol L−1, by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) with an inert atmosphere and pH 5.5 after the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures. Before analysis, the modified electrode was submitted to 20 differential pulse voltammograms with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1, pulse amplitude of 100 mV, and a step potential of 6 mV.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in 1 mmol. L−1 of K3[Fe(CN)6] / K4[Fe(CN)6] solution prepared in 1 mol L−1 KCl. The EIS study was performed with a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz employing a potentiostat/galvanostat model 206 N Autolab. The Randles–Sevcik equation has been used to calculate the electroactive surface areas of PCE bare and modified with nanostructures through the cyclic voltammograms obtained in 5 × 10−3 mol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution prepared in 1 mol L−1 KCl.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using the modified Hummers method with few modifications [46]. In a 500 mL round bottom flask, 1 g of graphite powder and 60 mL of H2SO4 were added. This solution was left in an ice bath (temperature < 10 °C) and under constant stirring for 15 minutes. At the end of the time, 3.5 g of KMnO4 was added over 15 minutes, and after adding the reagent, the new solution was left to rest for 4 hours without an ice bath and with constant stirring. After the reaction time, the system was diluted with 200 mL of H2O(dest), and the excess oxidant (Mn2O7) was eliminated by adding 10 mL of H2O2.

After dilution and oxidant elimination, the system was left to rest for 24 hours. After resting, the solution was transferred to a Büchner funnel with a quantitative paper filter, mounted on a kitasato, and connected to a vacuum pump, where it was washed with 500 mL of H2O(dest), 250 mL of HCl 10%, 250 mL of ethanol (EtOH) 95% and 500 mL of H2O(dest). Finally, the Graphite oxide (Gr-O) was collected and stored in a Schott flask with 95% EtOH or deionized H2O. Subsequently, aliquots of this material were diluted and exfoliated in an ultrasound bath for approximately 10 minutes, obtaining GO.

In a 125 mL round-bottom flask, it was added 48 mL of EtOH dried with a molecular sieve, 4 mL of Copper (II) nitrate (light blue color) stock solution of concentration 0.1 mol.L−1, and 1 mL of GO solution (light brown color) with 1mg.mL−1 concentration. This solution was then heated and stirred until 70 °C was reached. After this, N2 was purged for 5 minutes using the rubber stopper. After this period, 4 mL of CDot(N) (white color) was previously synthesized according to NAME et al. [47] were added. Again, the system was closed with the stopper, and during the one-hour reaction time, it was heated at 70 °C, with constant purging and stirring. Fig. 1 shows the synthesis flowchart and the color difference between the reactants and the final product.

After the reaction ended, the dark brown material was collected with the help of EtOH and kept in a closed bottle. The material was washed for further usage by adding hexane, stirring, and precipitating in a centrifuge. This was followed by removing hexane and adding EtOH again, leaving a dark brown material at the bottom of the flask. This material was named CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures.
Fig. 2 a shows the UV-Vis spectra of CDot(N), copper (II) nitrate, and GO. In contrast, Fig. 2b compares CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures before and after washing.

As can be seen, the spectrum of CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures (Fig. 2b) showed electronic transitions different from their precursors, indicating the formation of graphene hybrid nanostructures. In Fig. 2a, the bands of CDot(N) around 209, 240, and 270 nm are associated with the C=C sp2 bonds in CDot(N), electron transfer of defects because of functional groups, and the presence of oxidized species, respectively [48].

It was impossible to identify the band referring to the C=N bonds in the 338–460 nm range due to n → p* transitions [49] because nitrogen atoms are on the inner CDot(N) structure, not the surface. For the copper (II) nitrate precursor, the spectrum showed two bands around 243 and 273 nm, referring to the O2− (2p) → Cu2+ (3d) transitions between the metal and the ligand [50]. Finally, the GO spectrum showed two bands around 229 and 306 nm, referring to the electronic transitions π →π* resulting, respectively, from sp2 carbon atoms in the hexagonal structure and the electronic transitions of the functional groups, mainly carboxylic and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of graphene oxides [39,51].
In Fig. 2b, the intense band at 206 nm corresponds to π→π* transitions of C=C bonds of GO/CDot(N) in the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures [47].
The bands around 242 and 280 nm disappear after washing with hexane and EtOH, which could mean that excessive reactants or undesirable subproducts were washed away.

Fig. 3 shows HR-TEM micrographs with different magnitudes of the CDot(N), CuONPs/CDot(N), GO, and CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. The HR-TEM images of CDot(N) confirm the nanometric size of N-doped carbon dots (about 3 nm) with interplane distances at 0.32 and 0.2 nm refer to the carbon planes (002) and (100), confirming the presence of a crystalline structure, which agrees with several nanocarbon materials [52]. The micrograph of the CuONPs/Cdot(N) nanostructures showed a nanoneedle structure with an average diameter of about 4.5 nm. HR-TEM micrographs GO (graphene oxide) presented nanosheets with few layers besides wrinkles and folds. CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures present quasi-spherical forms with an average diameter of 4.8 nm. Different structures presented by CuONPs/CDot(N) nanostructures indicate that the synergic effect between GO and CuONPs/CDot(N) influenced the structure of CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. CDot(N) could not be identified in the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) micrographs due to the high contrast of the nanostructures of CuONPs.

Fig. 4 shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s, Cu2p3/2, Cu2p1/2, O1s, and N1s was obtained. The spin-orbit component C1s presented three peaks, about 284.6, 285.9, and 288.4, that referred, respectively, to carbon bonds with sp2 hybridization of graphene sheets and carbon dots core, C–N bonding in carbon dots (N-sp2-C) and to the C-O, N-sp3-C, and C=O double bindings [53]. The high-resolution spectra deconvolution of O1s identified four peaks with binding energies at 529.4, 531.2, 532.85, and 535.65 eV that refer, respectively, chemisorbed oxygen, oxygen vacancies, lattice oxygen, and hydrated metallic oxides or adsorbed O2 [54,55] associated with the CuO structure.

The spectra deconvolution of N1s identified two peaks at 399.3 eV, referring to pyrrolic-N peaks, which are N atoms on the edge of a carbon nanostructure in a conjugated system [56] and 406.82 eV, which probably refer to -NO2 species formation [57,58]. These results prove that CDot(N) is present in the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. XPS analysis shows that the Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 peaks at 962.05, 953.15, 941.15, 943.3, and 933.45 refer to Cu–O bonds of CuO with two different states of oxidations [53,59–61]. The binding energies at 962.05, 941.15, and 943.3 eV are satellite peaks at higher binding energies.
Absent peaks at 932.4 and 936.6 eV, associated with Cu2O and metallic Cu, indicate that the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures constituted specifically by CuO nanoparticles, in agreement with HR-TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectroscopy analysis and X-ray diffraction (XRD)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the Raman spectroscopy of GO and CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. The GO Raman spectrum showed two characteristic bands at 1339 and 1575 cm−1, denominated D and G bands, respectively. The D band is related to the defects of the carbon honeycomb structure with C-C sp3 bounds because of functional groups. The G band is associated with the carbon honeycomb structure with C-C sp2 bounds [22]. Therefore, the G-band can probe any defect in graphene's flat geometric structure induced by external perturbation.
The Raman spectra of the CDot(N)/GO/CuONPs nanostructures differ from those of the GO isolated due to four peaks at 278, 612, 1348, and 1595 cm−1.

The 278 and 612 cm−1 peaks are related to phonon modes having symmetries Ag and 2Bg, respectively, of the CuO present in the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures. Because the primitive unitary cell has four atoms, the CuO has 12 phonon branches and three optical modes Raman-active (Ag + 2Bg). Only the oxygen atoms move, with displacements perpendicular to Bg modes and horizontal to Ag [62]. A1g is related to the symmetric bending of the M–O bond in the tetrahedral MO4 group, and 2Bg is associated with the asymmetric bending of the M–O bond in the tetrahedral MO4 group [63]. These wavenumbers are slightly smaller than reported in the literature because they probably have a small grain size [64,65]. The D and G bands' characteristic signal for carbon had wavenumbers distorted and ID/IG, which is a correlation between the band D and G, where the integrity of the structure can be analyzed, showed a correlation near 1.00, indicating that almost no structural occurred to GO during the synthesis.

Fig. 6 shows that X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures presented different peaks. The graphene oxide produced presented a diffraction peak of 2θ value about 8°, referring to the plane (002) related to the honeycomb structure of carbon. The Peak with a 2θ value of about 26° corresponds to crystalline planes (110) of carbon dots. The other peaks with 2θ values about 12 and 17° probably correspond to N, O doped graphite structures [66,67] due to many group functionals in the structure.

The peaks with 2θ values about peaks indexed about 33° (110), 34° (
1 ¯
11), 38.3° (111), 51° (
2 ¯
02), 52° (020), 55° (202), 72° (203) confirming the formation of phase monoclinic CuO nanoparticles planes (JCPDS No. 045-0937) [68], in the agreement of XPS analysis. The possible reason for weaker diffraction peaks of CuO can be due to nanosize and the homogeneous distribution of CuONPs/CDot(N) onto the GO surface [69], as probed by HR-TEM. The XRD results also reveal that there is no formation of crystalline Cu2O.

CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures were employed in successive glyph determinations in PBS, pH 5.5, with concentrations varying from 0 to 22 nmol.L−1 (Fig. 8a). The PCE modified with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures showed excellent electrocatalytic response in the glyph determination, in which the current decreases linearly with the increase of the glyph concentration.

The calibration curve, Fig. 8b, showed an excellent electrocatalytic response, which the equation can be represented: I (A) = 0.1898 (± 4.56 × 10−3) – 3.47 × 10−4 (±4.6 × 10−9) [glyph] with n= nine and R2= 0.987. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated was 1.97 nmol. L−1 according to the IUPAC (3σ/slope ratio, where σ is the standard deviation of current of the 10-differential pulse voltammograms of the blank) [71]. The LOD value obtained by CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures is much smaller than the tolerable value of the Brazilian regulatory European Community glyph limit, which accepts 59.15 nmol. L−1 [72], and The United States, which is 4.14 µmol.L−1 in drinking water [73]. The CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures' glyph determination performance was compared to other modified electrodes (Table 1 ). The CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures showed less negative potential and better sensitivity than other electrodes. This better performance than other modified electrodes possibly occurred because of improved charge transfer and conductivity on the electrode surface due to the small size of CuONPS/CDot(N) and homogeneous distribution onto the GO sheet, as seen in EIS and HR-TEM images.

Considering the good electrocatalytic performance of the modified electrode in determining the glyph, the PCE modified with nanostructures nanohybrid can be used to determine the glyph in real samples.

Firstly, the electrocatalytic properties of the printed carbon electrode (PCE) bare and modified with different nanomaterials against the pesticide glyphosate (glyph) determination were studied. The measurements were performed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) pH 5.5 using the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. This pH shows a high electrocatalytic current and less potential at glyphosate determination by the modified electrode with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures compared to other pHs (3.5, 4.5, 6.5, and 7.0) (figure not shown). As can be seen in Fig. 9 , when the glyph is added to the PBS at a concentration of 2.5 nmol.L−1, the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures showed a better electrocatalytic response (more significant decrease in current (∆i= 79.1 µA) and potential less negative, E= -0.015 V) compared to electrodes modified with other nanomaterials in that it was added higher glyph concentration. Even though the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures present a small electrochemically active surface area (0.068 cm2) compared to PCE bare, and CuONPs/CDot(N)/PCE being 0.126, and 0.114 cm2, respectively. The small value of the electrochemically active surface area of GO/PCE (0.010 cm2) is because of the isolant properties of graphene oxide. Despite that, the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE have shown better electrocatalytic response for a small glyph concentration due to the synergic effect between nanostructure that improves the reactive site's number compared to the other nanomaterials.

The suggested mechanism for electrocatalytic interaction between glyphosate (non-electroactive molecule) and CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures is because the interaction of glyphosate dispersed in solution with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures occurs due to the formation of a Cu-glyph complex [74,75] and GO/CDot(N)-glyph, see Fig. 10 . Studied models [76] showed that there is a strong interaction between CDot(N) and copper nanoparticles, altering the nanohybrid's ability to receive and donate electrons through the Homo/Lumo orbitals, improving the catalytic properties. In this way, theoric calculus [77] demonstrated that the glyphosate molecules can bond between the amino, carboxylic, and phosphate groups and exhibit a strong affinity for Cu2+ (Fig. 10a). The Cu-glyph complex formation in the electrode-solution interface reduces electroactive sites and decreases the CuONPs oxidation peak current when the glyphosate concentration increases, allowing glyphosate electrocatalytic determination. In addition to the formation of the Cu-glyph complex, theoretical calculations also have shown that the functional groups present in CDot(N) can interact with glyph through hydrogen bonding (Fig. 10b) [78], diminishing solvent effect and increasing the interactions between CDot(N), GO, and CuONPs with glyphosate (Fig. 10c), resulting in an excellent electrocatalytic activity.

Table 2 shows the results of using the extrapolation method, performed by the standard addition technique, to determine glyphosate in tap water, with a pH of 6.7, by CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures using electrochemical cell contend three electrodes (Pt auxiliary and Ag/AgCl reference). Initially, without previous treatment, tap water analysis was performed. No electrocatalytic response was obtained, possibly because no glyphosate was present or in concentrations lower than the detection limit of the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures. Therefore, the tap water was spiked with glyphosate at the concentrations 0.010, 0.0125, and 0.015 μmol L−1, and the recovery results, determined by the calibration method using differential pulse voltammetry, are summarized in Table 2. The CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures determine glyphosate in real samples with recovery ranging from 95.2% to 114%. The measurements were performed in triplicate, and the sensor was kept stable and with good sensibility. The matrix effect occurred because some salts dissolved in the water. However, these salts do not significantly interfere with glyphosate determinations.

The bar graph shows the effectiveness of the CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures to determine glyphosate in the presence of different possible interferents, such as pesticides methylcarbamate, aldicarb, and fenitrothion, Fig. 11 . The selectivity study was performed in differential pulse voltammetry in PBS, pH 5.4, with the concentration of interferents 10x higher than glyphosate. The efficiency of the electrode in determining glyphosate about fenitrothion and carbamate probably occurs because the formation of the copper-glyphosate bond is favored due to glyphosate having more exposed oxidative groups compared to fenitrothion and carbamates and binding more easily to copper with an open shell d9—orbital with small energy [79]. Therefore, it can be said that the electrode didn't suffer significative interference and can be used to determine glyphosate in the presence of interferences.

The PCE modification with CuONPs/GO/CDot(N) nanostructures presented excellent reproducibility (100%), considering that 30 electrodes were prepared in all studies (approximately two months). CuONPs/GO/CDot(N)/PCE nanostructures have been kept stable after 10 cycles without significant changes in your electrocatalytic current (about 2% approximately) in the phosphate buffer solution (PBS), 0.1 mol L−1, with an inert atmosphere and pH 5.5, in the presence of 12.5 nmol L−1 of glyphosate.

Daniel Y. Tiba: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Thiago C. Canevari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
